text: The surgeon begins mobilization of the small bowel, carefully separating it from the presumed old pexy site over the sacral promontory on the right side.

The surgeon performs a repeat mobilization in the retro rectal plane, carefully navigating through scar tissue from previous surgeries. The dissection progresses below the scarring, revealing normal presacral fascia, which allows the plane to open up more easily.

The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection down to the pelvic floor, identifying the ureter. The dissection begins posteriorly and progresses laterally, aiming to divide the mid rectum rather than the rectosigmoid junction, as indicated in the original operative report.

The surgeon carefully navigates the medially dissected plane, encountering scarring from prior surgery. The ureters are visible and identified without the use of ureteral catheters, ensuring no injury to the bowel or adjacent structures.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels drawn up onto the left side of the colon conduit and carefully dissects them down to access the medially dissected plane. The ureter is visible, and a sponge is used to maintain the surgical field, indicating successful entry into the medial plane.

The surgeon continues the left-sided dissection down to the pelvic floor, carefully addressing posterior adhesions likely from previous pexy sutures. The adhesions are released easily, allowing further dissection down to the floor in preparation for the pexy.

The surgeon encounters scarring in the distal area, likely due to the anastomosis. Despite not visually identifying staples, the surgeon uses digital palpation to locate the anastomosis, ensuring the dissection extends as far distally as possible. The scarring is visible posteriorly, suggesting the location of the anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies a mesenteric hematoma, likely caused by a tear from the grasper. Pressure is applied to the area for five minutes to control bleeding, after which the surgeon resumes distal dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects into the rectovaginal septum, noting the absence of mesorectum and the direct contact between the sigmoid or descending colon wall and the back of the vagina, requiring meticulous technique due to the sticky tissue.
The surgeon leaves the right side lateral stalks intact during the dissection to assist with the pexy suture, while attempting to dissect as far down as possible.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, observing some bleeding from the mesentery. The bleeding appears non-expanding and non-pulsatile, allowing the surgeon to continue with the posterior mobilization without immediate intervention.

The surgeon completes the dissection down to the floor in the rectovaginal septum, ensuring thorough exposure for addressing rectal prolapse. Upon inspection, an expanding hematoma is observed, indicating a need for further intervention.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture, while the patient remains stable. The surgical field shows no visible complications despite the surgeon's expressed frustration.

After placing stitches, the surgeon observes no further expansion or bleeding in the area. On the left lateral aspect, there is a potential development, but it is unclear if it has dissected around to the other side. The surgeon checks perfusion in the area obscured by the hematoma, confirming that perfusion appears normal.

The surgeon observes active bleeding from the bowel and attempts focused cautery, which is ineffective. A clamp is applied to stop the bleeding temporarily. Hemostatic agents, including gel foam and thrombin, are applied directly to the bleeding site, which successfully controls the bleeding. A rigid sigmoidoscopy reveals pink mucosa with no mucosal bleeding, indicating a healthy bowel interior.

The surgeon observes active bleeding and places a focused suture directly in the area where clamping previously stopped the bleeding, aiming to control the hemorrhage despite concerns about potential bowel wall ischemia.

The surgeon examines the surgical site closely, observing a reduction in bleeding intensity. Using 3-0 silk sutures, the surgeon creates mattress sutures above and below the bleeding area in the mesentery, which is friable and does not hold sutures well. A new sheet of Surgicel is placed beneath these sutures to aid in hemostasis.
